body_txt: In most games (and Real Life , too), players can often get by without knowing every single rule. Some rules rarely come up, after all, and it would be insane to memorize them all, particularly for something as complex as a Tabletop Game ... ...unless you are the Rules Lawyer. Every gaming group seems to have one — that one player who memorized the entire rulebook cover-to-cover and seems to be more interested in the rules themselves than the actual game being played with them. Three common flavors of Rules Lawyer are: What unites all of these is a nigh-encyclopedic knowledge of every single aspect of the rules of the system. Where they differ is in their attitude and how they use that knowledge. The Obnoxious Rules Lawyer - also sometimes called a "Rule Shark" - is probably the most recognized kind and is the reason that the term "Rules Lawyer" is seen as a negative. They believe that if they can find a rule for something, the people in charge must allow them to do it, regardless of what effect it has on the story or other people's enjoyment of the game. They will attempt to exploit every loophole and every footnote they can. Most annoyingly, they tend to be deliberately selective in what rules they remember, and will conveniently fail to mention rules that don't benefit them. Such people will insist on Exact Words whenever arguing their case. The Dumb Rules Lawyer is incompetent instead of malicious . They believe the rules are there to be followed exhaustively and to the letter, and will constantly grind play to a halt to make sure that happens — even if it's obvious that doing so won't change the outcome , or doesn't make sense in-story . They won't allow any technique to be used that isn't specified in the rules, stifling creativity and causing the game to become rigid. If the Game Master decides to make some well-designed and balanced homebrews then this lawyer will start throwing a fit . Usually, the first rule these Rules Lawyers conveniently "forget" while making their arguments is Rule Zero : the Game Master is always right . Squashing them with this early is the best bet — attempting to argue about rules with them only encourages their behavior. If invoked, they might dare to argue that Rule Zero is an unwritten rule and is therefore not enforceable - fortunately, some rule systems are wise to this and do have a form of Rule Zero codified. However, Rules Lawyers aren't always a bad thing - having one can be a real asset to a gaming group too, and this is where the Helpful Rules Lawyer comes in. The Helpful Rules Lawyer knows the rulebook front to back, making them an invaluable game guide as they can quickly explain any aspect of the gameplay, no matter how obscure. This avoids the need to look up rules and helps the game to move along at a good pace. They can also easily spot cheating players and force them to play fair, and may be able to help out other players with options they may not have known about. They may also know about the potentially-wonky interactions of various expanded and optional rules sets and how to handle these, which frees the GM to focus on creating a rich world, vibrant characters, and compelling narrative for the players to interact with. The truly great players of this type will also insist on following the rules even to their own detriment, or otherwise ensure that they're not getting any benefits that the rules say they haven't earned. Rules Lawyers can also keep a misbehaving Game Master in check by preventing them from changing rules mid-game, which might happen if the GM is trying to force their own preferences onto the group. A Rules Lawyer can help to police such game abuse by pointing out when the GM is overstepping the bounds of the game. If a rule ever seems to be written rather verbosely, or explain things that seem like they should be common sense, it's the writers trying to stop people like this. Rules Lawyers are among the few people who Read the Fine Print , and will always use Exact Words to their advantage (at least until said advantage is closed by an Obvious Rule Patch ). When two Rules Lawyers at the table decide to butt heads, see My Rule Fu Is Stronger than Yours . In Real Life , a person trying to be a Rules Lawyer is usually countered by an authority figure invoking Rule Zero , Screw the Rules, I Make Them! , or even simply a Big "SHUT UP!" . In fantasy, The Fair Folk , The Trickster , and the Literal Genie are often Rules Lawyers. See also Puzzle Thriller , a style of story which revolves around characters finding creative ways to exploit or circumvent the rules of their universe. Jinx Game is something that they may inflict on others, or find loopholes to escape.
